question_text: An electromagnetic wave travels in vacuum along z direction: {tex}\vec E ({E_1\hat i} + {E_2} \hat j){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t). Choose the correct option from the following: The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c}\left(E_{1} {\mathbf{\hat i}}-E_{2} {\mathbf{\hat j}}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t). The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c}\left(E_{1} {\mathbf{\hat i}}-E_{2} {\mathbf{\hat j}}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t). The given electromagnetic field is circularly polarised. The given electromagnetic wave is plane polarised.
question_html:
An electromagnetic wave travels in vacuum along z direction: {tex}\vec E ({E_1\hat i} + {E_2} \hat j){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t). Choose the correct option from the following:

  1. The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c}\left(E_{1} {\mathbf{\hat i}}-E_{2} {\mathbf{\hat j}}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t).
  2. The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c}\left(E_{1} {\mathbf{\hat i}}-E_{2} {\mathbf{\hat j}}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t).
  3. The given electromagnetic field is circularly polarised.
  4. The given electromagnetic wave is plane polarised.
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. We are given that the electric field vector of an electromagnetic wave travels in a vacuum along z-direction as, {tex}\vec{E}=\left(E_{2} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) The magnitude of the electric and the magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are related as {tex}B_{0}=\frac{E_{0}}{c}{/tex} {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\vec{E}}{c}=\frac{E_{1} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}}{c}{/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) Also, {tex}\vec E{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are perpendicular to each other and the propagation of electromagnetic wave is perpendicular to {tex}\vec E{/tex} as well as {tex}\vec B{/tex} , so the given electromagnetic wave is plane polarized.
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.
We are given that the electric field vector of an electromagnetic wave travels in a vacuum along z-direction as,
{tex}\vec{E}=\left(E_{2} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}\right){/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
The magnitude of the electric and the magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are related as
{tex}B_{0}=\frac{E_{0}}{c}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\vec{E}}{c}=\frac{E_{1} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}}{c}{/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
Also, {tex}\vec E{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are perpendicular to each other and the propagation of electromagnetic wave is perpendicular to {tex}\vec E{/tex} as well as {tex}\vec B{/tex}, so the given electromagnetic wave is plane polarized.

question_text: An electromagnetic wave travelling along z-axis is given as: {tex}\vec E = \vec E_ {o}{/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t.). Choose the correct options from the following: The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c} \mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}=\frac{1}{\omega}(\mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}){/tex} The electromagnetic field can be written in terms of the associated magnetic field as {tex}\mathbf{\vec E}=c(\mathbf{\vec B} \times \mathbf{\vec k}){/tex} {tex}\mathbf{\vec k}, \mathbf{\vec E}=0, \mathbf{\vec k}, \mathbf{\vec B}=0{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}=0, \mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec B}=0{/tex}
question_html:
An electromagnetic wave travelling along z-axis is given as: {tex}\vec E = \vec E_ {o}{/tex} cos (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t.). Choose the correct options from the following:

  1. The associated magnetic field is given as {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=\frac{1}{c} \mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}=\frac{1}{\omega}(\mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}){/tex}
  2. The electromagnetic field can be written in terms of the associated magnetic field as {tex}\mathbf{\vec E}=c(\mathbf{\vec B} \times \mathbf{\vec k}){/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathbf{\vec k}, \mathbf{\vec E}=0, \mathbf{\vec k}, \mathbf{\vec B}=0{/tex}
  4. {tex}\mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec E}=0, \mathbf{\vec k} \times \mathbf{\vec B}=0{/tex}
solution_text: (a), (b) and (c) are correct. The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is always along the direction of vector product {tex}\vec{E} \times \vec{B}{/tex} . Refer to figure. {tex}\vec{B}=B \hat{j}=B(\hat{k} \times \hat{i}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{E}{c}(\hat{k} \times \hat{i}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{c}[k \times E \hat{i}]=\frac{1}{c}[\hat{k} \times \vec{E}]{/tex} (as {tex}\frac{E}{B}{/tex} = c) {tex}\vec{E}=E \hat{i}=c B(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=c(B \hat{j} \times \hat{k})=c(\vec{B} \times \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\hat{k} \cdot \vec{E}=\hat{k} \cdot(E \hat{i})=0{/tex} , {tex}\vec{k} \cdot \vec{B}=\hat{k} \cdot(B \hat{j})=0{/tex}
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (c) are correct.

  1. The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is always along the direction of vector product {tex}\vec{E} \times \vec{B}{/tex}. Refer to figure.
    {tex}\vec{B}=B \hat{j}=B(\hat{k} \times \hat{i}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{E}{c}(\hat{k} \times \hat{i}){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{c}[k \times E \hat{i}]=\frac{1}{c}[\hat{k} \times \vec{E}]{/tex} (as {tex}\frac{E}{B}{/tex} = c)
  2. {tex}\vec{E}=E \hat{i}=c B(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=c(B \hat{j} \times \hat{k})=c(\vec{B} \times \hat{k}){/tex}
  3. {tex}\hat{k} \cdot \vec{E}=\hat{k} \cdot(E \hat{i})=0{/tex}, {tex}\vec{k} \cdot \vec{B}=\hat{k} \cdot(B \hat{j})=0{/tex}

question_text: A charged particle oscillates about its mean equilibrium position with a frequency of 10 9 Hz. The electromagnetic waves produced: will have frequency of 10 9 Hz. will have frequency of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 H will have a wavelength of 0.3 m. fall in the region of radiowaves.
question_html:
A charged particle oscillates about its mean equilibrium position with a frequency of 109 Hz. The electromagnetic waves produced:

  1. will have frequency of 109 Hz.
  2. will have frequency of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 H
  3. will have a wavelength of 0.3 m.
  4. fall in the region of radiowaves.
solution_text: (a), (c) and (d) are correct. Here we are given the frequency by which the charged particles oscillates about its mean equilibrium position, it is equal to 10 9 . The frequency of electromagnetic waves produced by a charged particle is equal to the frequency by which it oscillates about its mean equilibrium position. So, the frequency of electromagnetic waves produced by the charged particle is v = 10 9 Hz. Wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{9}}{/tex} = 0.3 m The frequency of 10 9 Hz falls in the region of radiowaves.
solution_html:
(a), (c) and (d) are correct. 
Here we are given the frequency by which the charged particles oscillates about its mean equilibrium position, it is equal to 109. The frequency of electromagnetic waves produced by a charged particle is equal to the frequency by which it oscillates about its mean equilibrium position. So, the frequency of electromagnetic waves produced by the charged particle is v = 109 Hz.
Wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{9}}{/tex} = 0.3 m
The frequency of 109 Hz falls in the region of radiowaves.

subject: Physics
book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The source of electromagnetic waves can be a charge moving with a constant velocity. moving in a circular orbit. at rest. falling in an electric field.
question_html:
The source of electromagnetic waves can be a charge

  1. moving with a constant velocity.
  2. moving in a circular orbit.
  3. at rest.
  4. falling in an electric field.
solution_text: (b) and (d) are correct. Key concept: An electromagnetic wave can be produced by accelerated or oscillating charge. An oscillating charge is accelerating continuously, it will radiate electromagnetic waves continuously. Electromagnetic waves are also produced when fast-moving electrons are suddenly stopped by a metal target of high atomic number. Here, in option (b) charge is moving in a circular orbit. In circular motion, the direction of the motion of charge is changing continuously, thus it is an accelerated motion and this option is correct In option (d), the charge is falling in electric field. If a charged particle is moving in electric field it experiences a force or we can say it accelerates. We know an accelerating charge particle radiates electromagnetic waves. Hence option (d) is also correct. Also, we know that a charge starts accelerating when it falls in an electric field. Important points: In an atom an electron is circulating around the nucleus in a stable orbit although accelerating does not emit electromagnetic waves; it does so only when it jumps from a higher energy orbit to a lower energy orbit. A simple LC oscillator and energy source can produce waves of desired frequency.
solution_html:
(b) and (d) are correct.

Key concept:

  • An electromagnetic wave can be produced by accelerated or oscillating charge.
  • An oscillating charge is accelerating continuously, it will radiate electromagnetic waves continuously.
  • Electromagnetic waves are also produced when fast-moving electrons are suddenly stopped by a metal target of high atomic number.

Here, in option (b) charge is moving in a circular orbit. In circular motion, the direction of the motion of charge is changing continuously, thus it is an accelerated motion and this option is correct In option (d), the charge is falling in electric field. If a charged particle is moving in electric field it experiences a force or we can say it accelerates. We know an accelerating charge particle radiates electromagnetic waves. Hence option (d) is also correct. Also, we know that a charge starts accelerating when it falls in an electric field. Important points:

  • In an atom an electron is circulating around the nucleus in a stable orbit although accelerating does not emit electromagnetic waves; it does so only when it jumps from a higher energy orbit to a lower energy orbit.
  • A simple LC oscillator and energy source can produce waves of desired frequency.

question_text: An EM wave of intensity I falls on a surface kept in vacuum and exerts radiation pressure p on it. Which of the following are true? Radiation pressure is I/c if the wave is totally absorbed. Radiation pressure is I/c if the wave is totally reflected. Radiation pressure is 2I/c if the wave is totally reflected. Radiation pressure is in the range I/c < p < 2I/c for real surfaces.
question_html:
An EM wave of intensity I falls on a surface kept in vacuum and exerts radiation pressure p on it. Which of the following are true?

  1. Radiation pressure is I/c if the wave is totally absorbed.
  2. Radiation pressure is I/c if the wave is totally reflected.
  3. Radiation pressure is 2I/c if the wave is totally reflected.
  4. Radiation pressure is in the range I/c < p < 2I/c for real surfaces.
solution_text: (a), (c) and (d) are correct. Key concept: Radiation pressure (p) is the force exerted by the electromagnetic wave on the unit area of the surface, i.e., rate of change of momentum per unit area of the surface. Let us consider a surface exposed to electromagnetic radiation as shown in the figure. The radiation is falling normally on the surface. Further, the intensity of radiation is I and the area of surface exposed to radiation is A. E = Energy received by surface per second = I.A N = Number of photons received by surface per second {tex}N=\frac{E}{E_{\text {photon }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E \lambda}{h c}=\frac{I A \lambda}{h c}{/tex} Now, there are three cases possible which are as follows: Case I: Surface is perfectly reflecting {tex}\triangle{/tex} P one proton = Change in momentum {tex}=\frac{2 h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total force experienced F = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} P one proton {tex}=\frac{2 I A}{c}{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} P one proton {tex}=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} P one proton {tex}=\frac{I A}{\varepsilon}{/tex} Also, Pressure {tex}P=\frac{F}{A}=\frac{I}{c}{/tex} Hence radiation pressure is in the range {tex}\frac{I}{c}<p<\frac{2 I}{c}{/tex} for real surfaces.
solution_html:
(a), (c) and (d) are correct.

Key concept: Radiation pressure (p) is the force exerted by the electromagnetic wave on the unit area of the surface, i.e., rate of change of momentum per unit area of the surface. Let us consider a surface exposed to electromagnetic radiation as shown in the figure. The radiation is falling normally on the surface. Further, the intensity of radiation is I and the area of surface exposed to radiation is A.

E = Energy received by surface per second = I.A
N = Number of photons received by surface per second
{tex}N=\frac{E}{E_{\text {photon }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E \lambda}{h c}=\frac{I A \lambda}{h c}{/tex}
Now, there are three cases possible which are as follows:
Case I: Surface is perfectly reflecting 
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Pone proton = Change in momentum {tex}=\frac{2 h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total force experienced F = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}Pone proton {tex}=\frac{2 I A}{c}{/tex}
 {tex}\triangle{/tex}Pone proton {tex}=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = N {tex}\times{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}Pone proton {tex}=\frac{I A}{\varepsilon}{/tex}
Also, Pressure {tex}P=\frac{F}{A}=\frac{I}{c}{/tex}
Hence radiation pressure is in the range {tex}\frac{I}{c}<p<\frac{2 I}{c}{/tex} for real surfaces.

question_text: Why is the orientation of the portable radio with respect to the broadcasting station important?
question_html: Why is the orientation of the portable radio with respect to the broadcasting station important?
solution_text: Transmitted carrier wave signals are plane-polarized and if the intensity of the signal is poor then the receiving antenna of radio must be parallel to the component of either electric or magnetic field. Because energy is only due to amplitudes of electric and magnetic components in EM wave, the magnitude of amplitude is in particular direction perpendicular to each other and perpendicular to wave propagation.
solution_html: Transmitted carrier wave signals are plane-polarized and if the intensity of the signal is poor then the receiving antenna of radio must be parallel to the component of either electric or magnetic field. Because energy is only due to amplitudes of electric and magnetic components in EM wave, the magnitude of amplitude is in particular direction perpendicular to each other and perpendicular to wave propagation.

question_text: The charge on a parallel plate capacitor varies as q = q 0 cos 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex} t. The plates are very large and close together (area = A, separation = d). Neglecting the edge effects, find the displacement current through the capacitor?
question_html: The charge on a parallel plate capacitor varies as q = q0 cos 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t. The plates are very large and close together (area = A, separation = d). Neglecting the edge effects, find the displacement current through the capacitor?
solution_html:
The displacement current in a capacitor is equal to the conduction current of the capacitor.
Displacement current, Id​ = Ic
The displacement current through the capacitor is given by
Id = Ic = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex}........(i)
Here we are given, q = q0 cos 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t
Putting this value in Eq (i), we get
Id = Ic = - q0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}
Id = Ic = -2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}q0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t

question_text: A variable frequency a.c source is connected to a capacitor. How will the displacement current change with decrease in frequency?
question_html: A variable frequency a.c source is connected to a capacitor. How will the displacement current change with decrease in frequency?
solution_text: Capacitive reaction {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} ,where C is the capacitance and f is the frequency Hence, {tex}X_{C} \propto \frac{1}{f}{/tex} As frequency decreases, X C increases and the conduction current is inversely proportional to {tex}X_{C}\left(\because I \propto \frac{1}{X_{C}}\right){/tex} . It means the displacement current decreases as the conduction current is equal to the displacement current.
solution_html:
Capacitive reaction {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} ,where C is the capacitance and f is the frequency
Hence, {tex}X_{C} \propto \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
As frequency decreases, XC increases and the conduction current is inversely proportional to {tex}X_{C}\left(\because I \propto \frac{1}{X_{C}}\right){/tex}.
It means the displacement current decreases as the conduction current is equal to the displacement current.

question_text: The magnetic field of a beam emerging from a filter facing a floodlight is given by B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z - 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) T. What is the average intensity of the beam?
question_html: The magnetic field of a beam emerging from a filter facing a floodlight is given by B0= 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z - 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 t) T. What is the average intensity of the beam?
solution_text: The standard equation of magnetic field can be expressed as B = B 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t. We are given equation B = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (120 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z - 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t)T On comparing this equation with standard equation, we get B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T and The average intensity of the beam is given by :- {tex}I_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}} \cdot c=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{\left(12 \times 10^{-8}\right)^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 1.71 W/m 2
solution_html:
The standard equation of magnetic field can be expressed as B = B0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t.
We are given equation

question_text: Professor C.V Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it. Which property of EM waves was he exhibiting? Give one more example of this property.
question_html: Professor C.V Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it. Which property of EM waves was he exhibiting? Give one more example of this property.
solution_text: The properties of an electromagnetic wave are the same as other waves. Like other waves, an electromagnetic wave also carries energy and momentum. We know the dual nature of radiation and matter. since it carries momentum, an electromagnetic wave also exerts pressure called radiation pressure. This property of electromagnetic waves helped professor CV Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it.

Electromagnetic waves with wavelength

UV-rays Preserve food, sterilizing the surgical instruments, detecting the invisible writings, fingerprints, LASIK eye surgery etc. Visible light To see objects Infrared rays in physical therapy (To treat, muscular strain), for taking photography during the fog, haze, optical communication etc. Micro wave and radio wave In radar and telecommunication.

question_text: Show that the average value of radiant flux density S over a single period T is given by {tex}\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{c} \mu_{0}} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
question_html: Show that the average value of radiant flux density S over a single period T is given by {tex}\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{c} \mu_{0}} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Radiant flux density {tex}\vec{S}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}(\vec{E} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}=c^{2} \varepsilon_{0}(\vec{E} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\left[\because c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}\right]{/tex} Let electromagnetic waves be propagating along x-axis. If electric field vector of electromagnetic wave be along y-axis, then magnetic field vector be along z-axis. Therefore, E = E 0 cos (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) and B = B 0 cos (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) E {tex}\times{/tex} B = (E 0 {tex}\times{/tex} B 0 ) cos 2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) S = c 2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (E {tex}\times{/tex} B) = c 2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (E 0 {tex}\times{/tex} B 0 ) cos 2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) Average value of the magnitude of radiant flux density over complete cycle is S av = c 2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}\left|E_{0} \times B_{0}\right| \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T}{/tex} cos 2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S av = c 2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0} E_{0} B_{0} \times \frac{1}{T} \times \frac{T}{2}{/tex} As {tex}\left[\int_{0}^{T} \cos ^{2}(k x-\omega t) d t=\frac{T}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{c^{2}}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}\left(\frac{E_{0}}{c}\right){/tex} [As c = {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{c}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=\frac{1}{c^{2} \mu_{0}}{/tex} hence average value of radiant flux density S over a single period T is given by :- {tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{2 \mu_{0} c}{/tex} . Hence proved.
solution_html:
Radiant flux density
{tex}\vec{S}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}(\vec{E} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}=c^{2} \varepsilon_{0}(\vec{E} \times \vec{B}){/tex}  {tex}\left[\because c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}\right]{/tex}
Let electromagnetic waves be propagating along x-axis. If electric field vector of electromagnetic wave be along y-axis, then magnetic field vector be along z-axis. Therefore,
E = E0 cos (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
and B = B0 cos (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
E {tex}\times{/tex} B = (E0 {tex}\times{/tex} B0) cos2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
S = c2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (E {tex}\times{/tex} B)
= c2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (E0 {tex}\times{/tex} B0) cos2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
Average value of the magnitude of radiant flux density over complete cycle is
Sav = c2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0}\left|E_{0} \times B_{0}\right| \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T}{/tex}  cos2 (kx - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Sav = c2 {tex}\varepsilon_{0} E_{0} B_{0} \times \frac{1}{T} \times \frac{T}{2}{/tex}
As {tex}\left[\int_{0}^{T} \cos ^{2}(k x-\omega t) d t=\frac{T}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{c^{2}}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}\left(\frac{E_{0}}{c}\right){/tex}[As c = {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{c}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=\frac{1}{c^{2} \mu_{0}}{/tex}   hence average value of radiant flux density S over a single period T is given by :-
{tex}\Rightarrow S_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{2 \mu_{0} c}{/tex}. Hence proved.

question_text: Show that the radiation pressure exerted by an EM wave of intensity I on a surface kept in vacuum is {tex}\frac {I}{c}{/tex} .
question_html: Show that the radiation pressure exerted by an EM wave of intensity I on a surface kept in vacuum is {tex}\frac {I}{c}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let us consider a surface exposed to electromagnetic radiation. The radiation is falling normally on the surface. Further, intensity of radiation is I and area of surface exposed to radiation is A. E = Energy received by surface per second = I.A N = Number of photons received by surface per second {tex}N=\frac{E}{E_{\text {photon }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E \lambda}{h c}=\frac{I A \lambda}{h c}{/tex} Let the surface is perfectly absorbing, {tex}\triangle P_{\text {onephoton }}=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta P_{\text {one photon }}=\frac{I A}{c}{/tex} Also, Pressure {tex}P=\frac{F}{A}=\frac{I}{c}{/tex} , this is the required result.
solution_html:
Let us consider a surface exposed to electromagnetic radiation. The radiation is falling normally on the surface. Further, intensity of radiation is I and area of surface exposed to radiation is A.
E = Energy received by surface per second = I.A
N = Number of photons received by surface per second
{tex}N=\frac{E}{E_{\text {photon }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E \lambda}{h c}=\frac{I A \lambda}{h c}{/tex}
Let the surface is perfectly absorbing, {tex}\triangle P_{\text {onephoton }}=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta P_{\text {one photon }}=\frac{I A}{c}{/tex}
Also, Pressure {tex}P=\frac{F}{A}=\frac{I}{c}{/tex} , this is the required result.

question_text: What happens to the intensity of light from a bulb if the distance from the bulb is doubled? As a laser beam travels across the length of a room, its intensity essentially remains constant. What geometrical characteristic of LASER beam is responsible for the constant intensity which is missing in the case of light from the bulb?
question_html:
What happens to the intensity of light from a bulb if the distance from the bulb is doubled? As a laser beam travels across the length of a room, its intensity essentially remains constant.
What geometrical characteristic of LASER beam is responsible for the constant intensity which is missing in the case of light from the bulb?
solution_text: Bulb spreads its light in all ground spherically and symmetrically. So if the distance from the bulb is doubled, the surface area covered by radiations change from 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 to 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} (2r) 2 i.e. four times decreased in straight laser. But in a bulb it decreased by 4(4 {tex}\pi{/tex} ), i.e 16 {tex}\pi{/tex} decreased. So the intensity becomes one-fourth the initial value in straight line, since {tex}\left(I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex} and for spherical source {tex}\left(I \propto \frac{1}{4 \pi r^{2}}\right){/tex} . In case of laser, it does not spread in all directions. It passes only along a straight line. So its intensity remains same almost. Geometrical characteristics of LASER beam which is responsible for the constant intensity are: Monochromatic Coherent Highly collimated from all around source Unidirectional There characteristics are missing in the case of light from the bulb.
solution_html:
Bulb spreads its light in all ground spherically and symmetrically. So if the distance from the bulb is doubled, the surface area covered by radiations change from 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 to 4{tex}\pi{/tex}(2r)2 i.e. four times decreased in straight laser. But in a bulb it decreased by 4(4{tex}\pi{/tex}), i.e 16{tex}\pi{/tex} decreased. So the intensity becomes one-fourth the initial value in straight line, since {tex}\left(I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex} and for spherical source {tex}\left(I \propto \frac{1}{4 \pi r^{2}}\right){/tex}.
In case of laser, it does not spread in all directions. It passes only along a straight line. So its intensity remains same almost.
Geometrical characteristics of LASER beam which is responsible for the constant intensity are:

  1. Monochromatic
  2. Coherent
  3. Highly collimated from all around source
  4. Unidirectional

There characteristics are missing in the case of light from the bulb.

question_text: Even though an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} exerts a force q {tex}\vec E{/tex} on a charged particle yet the electric field of an EM wave does not contribute to the radiation pressure (but transfers energy). Explain.
question_html: Even though an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} exerts a force q{tex}\vec E{/tex} on a charged particle yet the electric field of an EM wave does not contribute to the radiation pressure (but transfers energy). Explain.
solution_text: The electric field of an electromagnetic wave is an oscillation field, which causes the force on a charged particle. This electric force averaged over an integral number of cycles is zero, since its direction changes every half cycle. Hence, the electric field is not responsible for radiation pressure though it transfers the energy. In fact, radiation pressure appears as a result of the action of the magnetic field of the wave on the electric currents induced by the electric field of the same wave.

question_html: An infinitely long thin wire carrying a uniform linear static charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} is placed along the z-axis (Fig). solution_text: The electric field due to infinitely long thin wire {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda }{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a} \hat{j}{/tex} Magnetic field due to the wire, {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}{/tex} Equivalent current flowing through the wire, i = {tex}\lambda{/tex} v Hence {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} \lambda v}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}{/tex} , poynting vector is given by :- {tex}\therefore \vec{S}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}[\vec{E} \times \vec{B}]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a} \hat{j} \times \frac{\mu_{0} \lambda v}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{S}=\frac{\lambda^{2} v}{4 \pi^{2} \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}(\hat{j} \times \hat{i})=-\frac{\lambda^{2} v}{4 \pi^{2} \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
The electric field due to infinitely long thin wire
{tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\lambda }{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a} \hat{j}{/tex}
Magnetic field due to the wire, {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}{/tex}
Equivalent current flowing through the wire, i = {tex}\lambda{/tex}v
Hence {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} \lambda v}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}{/tex} , poynting  vector is given by :-
{tex}\therefore \vec{S}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}[\vec{E} \times \vec{B}]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0}}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a} \hat{j} \times \frac{\mu_{0} \lambda v}{2 \pi a} \hat{i}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{S}=\frac{\lambda^{2} v}{4 \pi^{2} \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}(\hat{j} \times \hat{i})=-\frac{\lambda^{2} v}{4 \pi^{2} \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}} \hat{k}{/tex}

Now using Ohm's law, the conduction current density {tex}J^{c}= rac{E}{ ho}= rac{1}{ ho} rac{V_{0}}{d}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t)
{tex}\Rightarrow J^{c}= rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t) {tex}=J_{0}^{c}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t)
Hence, {tex}J_{0}^{c}= rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}{/tex}
The displacement current density is given as
{tex}J^{d}=\varepsilon rac{d E}{d t}=\varepsilon rac{d}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left[ rac{V_{0}}{d} \sin (2 \pi u t) ight]{/tex}
{tex}= rac{\varepsilon 2 \pi v V_{0}}{d} \cos (2 \pi u t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow J^{d}=J_{0}^{d} \cos (2 \pi u t){/tex}
where, {tex}J_{0}^{d}= rac{2 \pi u \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad rac{J_{0}^{d}}{J_{0}^{c}}= rac{ rac{2 \pi u \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}}{ rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}}=2 \pi u\varepsilon ho{/tex}
{tex}=2 \pi imes 80 \varepsilon_{0} u imes 0.25{/tex} {tex}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} u imes 10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow rac{J_{0}^{d}}{J_{0}^{c}}= rac{10 imes 4 imes 10^{8}}{9 imes 10^{9}}= rac{4}{9}{/tex} ,this is required result.

The induced electric field at a distance s from the wire inside the cable is {tex}\vec E {/tex} (s, t) {tex}=\mu_{0} I_{0} v \cos (2 \pi v t){/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} Calculate the displacement current density inside the cable. Compare the conduction current I 0 with the displacement current {tex}I_{0}^{d}{/tex} .
question_html:
A long straight cable of length l is placed symmetrically along z-axis and has radius a(<<l). The cable consists of a thin wire and a co-axial conducting tube. An alternating current I(t) = Io sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt) flows down the central thin wire and returns along the co-axial conducting tube. The induced electric field at a distance s from the wire inside the cable is {tex}\vec E {/tex} (s, t) {tex}=\mu_{0} I_{0} v \cos (2 \pi v t){/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}

  1. Calculate the displacement current density inside the cable.
  2. Integrate the displacement current density across the crosssection of the cable to find the total displacement current Id.
  3. Compare the conduction current I0 with the displacement current {tex}I_{0}^{d}{/tex}.
solution_text: We are given, the induced electric field at a distance r from the wire inside the cable. {tex}\vec E{/tex} (s, t) {tex}=\mu_{0}{/tex} I 0 v cos (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt) In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} The displacement current density is given by {tex}\vec{J}_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \vec{E}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}{/tex} [ {tex}=\mu_{0}{/tex} I 0 v cos (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt) In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} ] {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \mu I_{0} v \frac{d}{d t}{/tex} cos [2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt] In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{c^{2}} I_{0} v^{2} 2 \pi{/tex} [-sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt] In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}} 2 \pi I_{0}{/tex} sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} 2 \pi I_{0}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \sin 2 \pi v t \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{J}_{d}=\frac{2 \pi I_{0}}{\lambda^{2}}{/tex} In {tex}\frac{a}{s} \sin 2 \pi v t \hat{k}{/tex} Total displacement current, I d {tex}=\int J_{d} 2 \pi s d s{/tex} {tex}I^{d}=\int_{0}^{a}\left(\frac{2 \pi I_{0}}{\lambda^{2}}\right.{/tex} In {tex}\left.\frac{a}{s} \sin 2 \pi v t\right) 2 \pi s d s{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{a}\left[\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda^{2}} I_{0} \int_{s=0}^{a}\right.{/tex} In {tex}\left.\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \text {sds} \sin 2 \pi v t\right] \times 2 \pi{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \int_{0}^{a}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \frac{1}{2} d\left(s^{2}\right) \cdot \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0}{/tex} {tex}\sin 2 \pi v t \int_{0}^{a}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \cdot d\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a^{2}}{4}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex} I 0 sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt {tex}\times{/tex} (1) {tex}\left[\because \int_{s=0}^{a} \ln \left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2} d\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2}=1\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I^{d}=\frac{a^{2}}{4}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I^{d}=\left(\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex} The displacement current, {tex}I_{d}=\left(\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex} I 0 sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt = {tex}I_{0}^{d}{/tex} sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vt Here, {tex}I_{0}^{d}=\left(\frac{a \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{I_{0}^{d}}{I_{0}}=\left(\frac{a \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. We are given, the induced electric field at a distance r from the wire inside the cable.
    {tex}\vec E{/tex} (s, t) {tex}=\mu_{0}{/tex} I0 v cos (2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt) In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    The displacement current density is given by {tex}\vec{J}_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \vec{E}}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}{/tex} [{tex}=\mu_{0}{/tex} I0 v cos (2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt) In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}]
    {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \mu I_{0} v \frac{d}{d t}{/tex} cos [2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt] In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{c^{2}} I_{0} v^{2} 2 \pi{/tex} [-sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt] In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}} 2 \pi I_{0}{/tex} sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt In {tex}\left(\frac{s}{a}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} 2 \pi I_{0}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \sin 2 \pi v t \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{J}_{d}=\frac{2 \pi I_{0}}{\lambda^{2}}{/tex} In {tex}\frac{a}{s} \sin 2 \pi v t \hat{k}{/tex}
  2. Total displacement current, Id {tex}=\int J_{d} 2 \pi s d s{/tex}

    {tex}I^{d}=\int_{0}^{a}\left(\frac{2 \pi I_{0}}{\lambda^{2}}\right.{/tex} In {tex}\left.\frac{a}{s} \sin 2 \pi v t\right) 2 \pi s d s{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_{0}^{a}\left[\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda^{2}} I_{0} \int_{s=0}^{a}\right.{/tex} In {tex}\left.\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \text {sds} \sin 2 \pi v t\right] \times 2 \pi{/tex}
    {tex}=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \int_{0}^{a}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \frac{1}{2} d\left(s^{2}\right) \cdot \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex}
    {tex}=\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0}{/tex} {tex}\sin 2 \pi v t \int_{0}^{a}{/tex} In {tex}\left(\frac{a}{s}\right) \cdot d\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{a^{2}}{4}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex} I0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt {tex}\times{/tex} (1) {tex}\left[\because \int_{s=0}^{a} \ln \left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2} d\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^{2}=1\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore I^{d}=\frac{a^{2}}{4}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow I^{d}=\left(\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0} \sin 2 \pi v t{/tex}
  3. The displacement current,
    {tex}I_{d}=\left(\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex} I0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt = {tex}I_{0}^{d}{/tex} sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}vt
    Here, {tex}I_{0}^{d}=\left(\frac{a \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2} I_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{I_{0}^{d}}{I_{0}}=\left(\frac{a \pi}{\lambda}\right)^{2}{/tex}

question_text: A plane EM wave travelling in vacuum along z direction is given by {tex}\vec E{/tex} = E 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\hat i{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} = B 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\hat j{/tex} Evaluate {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} over the rectangular loop 1234 shown in Fig. Evaluate {tex}\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{s}{/tex} over the surface bounded by loop 1234. Use equation {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-d \phi_{3}}{d t}{/tex} to prove {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=\mathrm{c}{/tex} . By using similar process and the equation {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} {tex}=\mu_{0} I+\varepsilon_{0} \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\mathbf{c}=\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{0}}}}{/tex} .
question_html:
A plane EM wave travelling in vacuum along z direction is given by {tex}\vec E{/tex} = E0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\hat i{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} = B0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\hat j{/tex}

  1. Evaluate {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} over the rectangular loop 1234 shown in Fig.
  2. Evaluate {tex}\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{s}{/tex} over the surface bounded by loop 1234.
  3. Use equation {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-d \phi_{3}}{d t}{/tex} to prove {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=\mathrm{c}{/tex}.
  4. By using similar process and the equation {tex}\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d} \mathbf{l}{/tex} {tex}=\mu_{0} I+\varepsilon_{0} \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\mathbf{c}=\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{0}}}}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let electromagnetic wave is propagating along z-axis, in this case electric field vector {tex}(\vec E){/tex} be along x-axis and magnetic field vector {tex}(\vec B){/tex} along y-axis, i.e., {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} The line integral of {tex}\vec E {/tex} over the closed rectangular path 1234 in x-z plane of the figure is given by , {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int_{1}^{2} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{2}^{3} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_{3}^{4} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{4}^{1} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{1}^{2}{/tex} E.dl cos 90° + {tex}\int_{2}^{3}{/tex} E 0 dl cos 0° + {tex}\int_{3}^{4}{/tex} E.dl cos 90° {tex}\int_{4}^{1} {/tex} E.dl cos 180° {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = E 0 h [sin (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) ...(i) Now let us evaluate {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex} , let us consider the rectangle 1234 to be made of small strips .One small strip of area ds = hdz each , is shown in fig;- {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex} {tex}=\int{/tex} B.ds cos 0 = {tex}=\int{/tex} B.ds {tex}=\int_{Z_{1}}^{Z_{2}}{/tex} B0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) hdz {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}=\frac{-B_{0} h}{k}{/tex} [cos (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - cos (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] ...(ii) We are given that {tex}\oint E \cdot d l=\frac{-d \phi_{B}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint B \cdot d s{/tex} Substituting the values from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get E 0 h [sin (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] {tex}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left[\frac{B_{0} h}{k}\right.{/tex} cos (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] {tex}=\frac{B_{0} h}{k} \omega{/tex} [sin (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] {tex}\Rightarrow E_{0}=\frac{B_{0} \omega}{k}=B_{0} c{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{\omega}{k}=c\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c{/tex} For evaluating {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} , let us consider a loop 1234 in y-z plane as shown in figure given below. {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int_{1}^{2} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_{2}^{3} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{3}^{4} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} l+\int_{4}^{1} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{1}^{2}{/tex} B.dl cos 0° + {tex}\int_{2}^{3}{/tex} B.dl cos 90° + {tex}\int_{3}^{4}{/tex} B.dl cos 180° + {tex}\int_{4}^{1} {/tex} cos 90° {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = B 0 h [sin (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] ...(iii) Now to evaluate {tex}\phi_{E}=\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex} , let us consider the rectangle 1234 to be made of strips and area of each strips is =hds each , shown in fig:- {tex}\phi_{E}=\int \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=\int{/tex} Eds cos 0 = {tex}\int{/tex} Eds {tex}=\int_{z_{1}}^{z}{/tex} E 0 sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) hdz {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=-\frac{E_{0} h}{k}{/tex} [cos (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] {tex}\therefore \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}=\frac{E_{0} h \omega}{k}{/tex} [sin (kz 1 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) - sin (kz 2 - {tex}\omega{/tex} t)] ...(iv) Let {tex}\oint B \cdot d l=\mu_{0}\left(I+\frac{\varepsilon_{o} d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right){/tex} where I is the = conduction current = 0 in vacuum {tex}\therefore \oint B \cdot d l=\mu_{0} \varepsilon \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} Using relations obtained in Eqs. (iii) and (iv) and simplifying, we get {tex}B_{0}=E_{0} \frac{\omega \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}} \frac{\omega}{k}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} But {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c{/tex} and {tex}\omega{/tex} = ck {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c.c {tex}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{c^2}={1\over \mu_0\epsilon_0}{/tex} therefore c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex} , hence we get the desired results.
solution_html:
1. Let electromagnetic wave is propagating along z-axis, in this case electric field vector {tex}(\vec E){/tex} be along x-axis and magnetic field vector {tex}(\vec B){/tex} along y-axis, i.e., {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{j}{/tex}

    The line integral of {tex}\vec E {/tex} over the closed rectangular path 1234 in x-z plane of the figure is given by ,
    {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int_{1}^{2} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{2}^{3} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_{3}^{4} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{4}^{1} \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_{1}^{2}{/tex} E.dl cos 90° + {tex}\int_{2}^{3}{/tex} E0dl cos 0° + {tex}\int_{3}^{4}{/tex} E.dl cos 90° {tex}\int_{4}^{1} {/tex} E.dl cos 180°
    {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = E0h [sin (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) ...(i)
  2. Now let us evaluate {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex}, let us consider the rectangle 1234 to be made of small strips .One small strip of area  ds = hdz each , is shown in fig;-

    {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex} {tex}=\int{/tex} B.ds cos 0 =  {tex}=\int{/tex} B.ds
    {tex}=\int_{Z_{1}}^{Z_{2}}{/tex} B0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) hdz
    {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}=\frac{-B_{0} h}{k}{/tex} [cos (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - cos (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)] ...(ii)
  3. We are given that  {tex}\oint E \cdot d l=\frac{-d \phi_{B}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint B \cdot d s{/tex}
    Substituting the values from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
    E0h [sin (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)]
    {tex}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left[\frac{B_{0} h}{k}\right.{/tex} cos (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)]
    {tex}=\frac{B_{0} h}{k} \omega{/tex} [sin (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow E_{0}=\frac{B_{0} \omega}{k}=B_{0} c{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{\omega}{k}=c\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c{/tex}
  4. For evaluating {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex}, let us consider a loop 1234 in y-z plane as shown in figure given below.

    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int_{1}^{2} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_{2}^{3} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{3}^{4} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} l+\int_{4}^{1} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_{1}^{2}{/tex} B.dl cos 0° + {tex}\int_{2}^{3}{/tex} B.dl cos 90° + {tex}\int_{3}^{4}{/tex} B.dl cos 180° + {tex}\int_{4}^{1} {/tex} cos 90°
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = B0h [sin (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)] ...(iii)
    Now to evaluate {tex}\phi_{E}=\int \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{s}{/tex}, let us consider the rectangle 1234 to be made of strips and  area of each strips is =hds each , shown in fig:-
     
    {tex}\phi_{E}=\int \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=\int{/tex} Eds cos 0 = {tex}\int{/tex} Eds
    {tex}=\int_{z_{1}}^{z}{/tex} E0 sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) hdz
    {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=-\frac{E_{0} h}{k}{/tex}[cos (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)]
    {tex}\therefore \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}=\frac{E_{0} h \omega}{k}{/tex}  [sin (kz1 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) - sin (kz2 - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)] ...(iv)
    Let {tex}\oint B \cdot d l=\mu_{0}\left(I+\frac{\varepsilon_{o} d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right){/tex} where  I is the  = conduction current
    = 0 in vacuum
    {tex}\therefore \oint B \cdot d l=\mu_{0} \varepsilon \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex}
    Using relations obtained in Eqs. (iii) and (iv) and simplifying, we get
    {tex}B_{0}=E_{0} \frac{\omega \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}} \frac{\omega}{k}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    But {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c{/tex} and {tex}\omega{/tex} = ck {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c.c {tex}=\frac{1}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} 

           {tex}{c^2}={1\over \mu_0\epsilon_0}{/tex}therefore c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex} , hence we get the desired results.

  1. The average energy density of the wave is given by {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
  2. The time averaged intensity of the wave is given by {tex}I_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{c} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Total energy carried by electromagnetic wave is due to electric field vector and magnetic field vector. In electromagnetic wave, E and B vary from point to point and from moment to moment. The energy density due to electric field E is given by:- {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} The energy density due to magnetic field B is given by: {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} The total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is given by: u = u E + u B {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Given that The electric field vector and magnetic field vector be represented by E = E 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) B = B 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) The values of E 2 and B 2 vary from point to point and from moment to moment. Hence, the effective values of E 2 and B 2 are their time averages over complete cycle. We know, {tex}\left\langle\sin ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left\langle\cos ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, the time average value of E 2 over complete cycle is given by, {tex}\left\langle B^{2}\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{T}\left[B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)\right]^{2} d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} The time average of energy density over complete cycle is given by- {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{0}\left(\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} We know {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permeability, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permittivity, E 0 and B 0 = Amplitudes of electric field and magnetic field vectors The time average of energy density due to magnetic field B is {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\left(E_{0}^{2} / c^{2}\right)}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}} \times \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Hence, u B = u E ; the time average of energy density due to magnetic field. {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Time average intensity of the wave is given by: I av = u av c {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Total energy carried by electromagnetic wave is due to electric field vector and magnetic field vector. In electromagnetic wave, E and B vary from point to point and from moment to moment.
    The energy density due to electric field E is given by:-
    {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
    The energy density due to magnetic field B is given by:
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    The total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is given by:
    u =  uE + uB {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    Given that The electric field vector and magnetic field vector be represented by
    E = E0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
    B = B0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
    The values of E2 and B2 vary from point to point and from moment to moment. Hence, the effective values of E2 and B2 are their time averages over complete cycle.
    We know, {tex}\left\langle\sin ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    and {tex}\left\langle\cos ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Hence, the time average value of E2 over complete cycle is given by,
    {tex}\left\langle B^{2}\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{T}\left[B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)\right]^{2} d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    The time average of energy density over complete cycle is given by-
    {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{0}\left(\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
  2. We know {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex}
    where {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permeability,  {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permittivity, E0 and B0 = Amplitudes of electric field and magnetic field vectors
    The time average of energy density due to magnetic field B is
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\left(E_{0}^{2} / c^{2}\right)}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}} \times \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    Hence, uB = uE; the time average of energy density due to magnetic field.
    {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    Time average intensity of the wave is given by:
    Iav = uavc {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
